text: Modern globalization, the international hierarchy of power, and most development frameworks remain tethered to the pursuit of economic growth. Yet, this reliance on Gross Domestic Product (GDP) is increasingly under fire for its social and ecological fallout. Rising global output is fundamentally linked to the overconsumption of energy and natural resources, threatening the very ecological systems that sustain life. In response, a diverse range of post-growth theories has emerged to offer alternative visions for the future. This article provides a comparative analysis of three influential schools of thought—steady-state economics, degrowth, and the well-being economy—tracing their intellectual origins, evolution, and real-world policy influence. By examining the tensions and synergies between these approaches, the paper seeks to advance the debate on how to effectively dismantle growth-centric paradigms in the international arena and move toward a system that prioritizes human and planetary health.
